behaviors.description: Generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type) from the description.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The signature is produced by a real LLM call. Mocking the LLM to return a hardcoded response only tests the extraction/parsing layer, not whether the model reliably generates the right signature. LLM-DEP: Correctness is entirely dependent on what the real LLM emits — a different prompt phrasing, temperature, or model version can cause the signature to drift. The failure mode is non-deterministic LLM output, not a deterministic parsing bug.
behaviors.test_description: Send a natural-language description with a precisely specified interface (e.g., `def add(a: int, b: int) -> int`) to the real LLM-backed generation service. Parse the returned code and assert that the function name, parameter names, type annotations, and return type all match the spec. Run 5+ times to catch non-deterministic failures.

behaviors.description: Generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: 'First or subsequent attempt' means the full generate→test→refine loop may need to run. Stopping after a single LLM call cannot validate the 'subsequent attempt' path. LLM-DEP: Whether any generated implementation actually passes a non-trivial test suite depends on real LLM output quality — mocking this away eliminates the behavior entirely. The failure mode only manifests when the real LLM, real test runner, and refinement prompt all interact correctly across iterations.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a function description with a realistic test suite to the full service backed by a real LLM. Allow up to 3 attempts to run. Assert that by the final attempt, the returned implementation passes all test cases when re-executed. Confirm the 'subsequent attempt' path by choosing a function that a single generation round commonly fails (e.g., a sorting function with edge-case tests).

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes the failure messages in the next LLM prompt.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is a wiring bug at the module boundary between the test runner and the prompt builder — specifically, whether the orchestration layer captures failure output and injects it into the next prompt. This cannot be caught by a Unit test of either module alone. MOCK-HIDE: We can mock the LLM (so call 1 returns broken code) and mock the test runner (so it returns realistic failure strings) without losing coverage of this specific bug. LLM-DEP does not apply: we are inspecting the prompt contents, not evaluating LLM output quality. REAL-INFRA: Real subprocess/code execution is not needed to verify prompt construction.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the orchestration module against a stub LLM client (first call returns syntactically valid but logically wrong code) and a stub test runner (returns a realistic failure message string). After one failing cycle, capture the prompt sent on the second LLM call and assert it contains the exact failure message text returned by the test runner.

behaviors.description: The service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error containing all three attempts and their test results.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode spans the orchestration loop and the result-accumulation logic — a single function boundary test cannot verify that state from all three iterations is collected and returned. The bug surface is the handoff between the iteration controller, the attempt-state store, and the final error builder. Unit tests of any one of those in isolation miss inter-module accumulation bugs. LLM-DEP does not apply: both the LLM and test runner can be stubbed (LLM always returns broken code; runner always returns failures) without hiding this behavior. REAL-INFRA: No OS or network dependency is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the service with a stub LLM that always returns syntactically valid but test-failing code and a stub test runner that always returns failures. Invoke the service and assert: (a) exactly 3 LLM calls were made, (b) the returned error structure contains 3 attempt records, (c) each record includes the generated code and the corresponding test failure output from that iteration.

behaviors.description: The full generate→test→refine→test loop produces a working implementation for a function a single LLM call typically gets wrong.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: This behavior explicitly requires multiple agentic steps in sequence: at least one failed generation, one refinement informed by test failures, and a subsequent passing test run. No single LLM call test can exercise the refinement feedback cycle. LLM-DEP: Whether the loop converges depends on the real model's ability to improve from failure feedback — a mock LLM that returns correct code on call 2 by construction tells us nothing about real model behavior. The specific value of this test is demonstrating that the feedback loop actually helps, which requires real non-deterministic LLM behavior across iterations.
behaviors.test_description: Choose a canonical function where single-shot generation reliably fails (e.g., a function with a non-obvious edge case like correct handling of empty input or integer overflow). Submit it with its test suite to the full service using a real LLM. Assert that the final result is marked successful, the passing implementation satisfies all test cases when re-run independently, and at least one prior attempt in the history was recorded as failing.
